Journal of Air Transport Management

journal homepage: www.elsevier .com/locate/ ja ir t raman
An investigation of air accidents in Nigeria using the Human Factors
Analysis and Classification System (HFACS) framework
Adebukola Yewande Daramola*
Economic Policy Research Department, Nigerian Institute of Social & Economic Research, PMB 5, UI P O, Ibadan, Nigeria
Keywords:
Accidents
Fatalities
HFACS
Errors
Nigeria
* Tel.: þ234 802 345 1396.
E-mail address: bukidaramola@yahoo.co.uk.

0969-6997/$ e see front matter  2013 Elsevier Ltd.
http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jairtraman.2013.11.004
a b s t r a c t

Increasing air traffic growth has been achieved along with substantial improvement in safety globally.
While air traffic is equally growing in Nigeria, safety levels do not appear to be growing side by side. This
was gleaned from the spate of accidents and fatalities recorded in the last couple of decades. The study
therefore set out to assess safety performance in Nigeria’s air transport industry by comparing accidents
and fatality rates with global average levels during the period 1985e2008. A content analysis of the
accident reports was done using the Human Factors Analysis Classification System (HFACS) as a con-
ceptual framework; this was augmented with results of industry experts assessment of the Nigerian
aviation industry. Their assessments were also discussed in the context of the conceptual framework.
Accidents and passenger fatality rates in Nigeria were higher than global average figures for most of the
years included in the analysis period. Findings on aircraft ages show that these are also higher than world
average levels. The aviation industry experts’ assessment presented various challenges which include
inadequate airport facilities, absence of timely meteorological information and dearth of skilled
personnel in Nigeria’s aviation industry. The content analysis of the accident reports using the HFACS
shows that skill based errors; physical environment and inadequate supervision are the most frequently
occurring categories influencing accident occurrences. The Chi-square and Fishers’s test used to analyze
significant relationships in the HFACS categories obtained in the accident reports showed five pairs of
significant associations between adjacent categories. Based on these associations, Supervisory
Violations:-Crew Resource Management:- Decision Errors path is deemed the most potent for accident
occurrences. Findings from the research point to the need to address personnel skill, physical environ-
ment issues (mostly weather related) and supervisory competence.

 2013 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
1. Introduction

Globally, air transport industries have a strong tradition of giv-
ing top priority to safety due to the life critical nature of its oper-
ations. The aviation industry is therefore regarded as a safety
critical industry. Fortunately, in addition to improved traffic
growth, productivity and cost reductions, a substantial improve-
ment in the safety of air travel has been achieved globally in the last
couple of decades. The number of passenger fatalities per 100
million passenger kilometers flown has fallen from 0.08 in 1980 to
0.03 in 1990 down to 0.02 in 1999 (ICAO,1981,1991, 2000). Over the
years 2000e2007, passenger death risk on scheduled flights in
developed nations like the U.S., Japan and Ireland was 1 in 14
million, for emerging nations, death risk per flight was 1 in 2
million; while in less economically advanced developing-world
All rights reserved.
countries (includes much of Africa) the death risk per flight was 1
in 800,000 (Barnett, 2010).

Global accident rates in the last decade have also been low and
averaged about four accidents per million flights (ICAO, 2011a). For
International Air Transport Association (IATA) member airlines,
aviation accidents have become even rarer with the probability of
one occurrence in 2.4 million flights. This translates to over 7000
years of accident free travel for a passengerwho boards a flight each
day IATA (2012).

Statistics on global accident rates therefore conceal regional
disparities between developed and developing nations; and
because developed nations account for the largest shares of air
passenger traffic globally, increased safety trends gleaned from
declining global accident and passenger fatality rates reflect more,
the performance of developed countries air transport industries.
Table 1 demonstrates this fact; it shows the number of accidents
per million departures for regions and the world (2006e2011).

Table 1 shows that air accident rates over the period 2005e2011
have been higher in Africa than in other regions of the world. Africa



40 A.Y. Daramola / Journal of Air Transport Management 35 (2014) 39e50

Table 1
Accident rates (World and Regions).

Oceania N/America LAC Europe Asia Africa World

2005 5.7 3.6 7.5 3.2 4.6 12.3 4.4
2006 3.4 3.0 6.9 4.1 4.2 15.3 4.1
2007 4.5 2.7 7.0 4.7 4.5 13.9 4.2
2008 4.5 3.0 11.5 4.3 4.5 12.7 4.8
2009 3.5 2.5 6.5 2.9 5.5 13.7 4.1
2010 6.9 3.2 6.6 3.5 3.4 19.3 4.2
2011 4.7 3.5 5.7 5.5 2.9 7.9 4.2

The significance of bold is to draw attention to Africa as a region and to highlight
higher than average global figures that was pervasive throughout the period.
Source: ICAO, 2011a.
consistently recorded higher accident rates than world averages.
Findings from Barnett’s study show that passengers who fly in
developing world countries (such as Africa) face 13 times the risk of
being killed in an air accident as passengers in the developed world
(Barnett, 2010). In 2010, Africa accounted for three per cent of
global airline traffic but had 14 per cent of the world’s aviation
accidents; this contrast with Oceania which equally accounted for
three percent of global air passenger traffic but recorded only 4
percent of the world’s aviation accidents in the same year (ICAO,
2011b). Effective state safety oversight capabilities, as measured
by the Universal Safety Oversight Audit Program (USOAP) of ICAO
shows average effective implementation values of USOAP protocols
to be lowest for Africa (41 percent) and Oceania (47 percent). These
are the two regions which fall below the worldwide average of 60
percent (ICAO, 2011b).

The study by Barnett in 2010 specifically notes that Nigeria has
one of the poorest safety records in the world. In Nigeria, the oc-
currences of air disasters were quite regular between 2003 and
2008, with eleven accidents and incidents involving 335 fatalities
(Samiplus, 2008). Major air accidents have equally been recorded in
2010 and 2012 for Nigeria.

Against the background of issues highlighted, this study has the
overall objective of analyzing the trend of aviation accidents in
Nigeria and to examine the causal factors using a human factors
approach. The study attempts to fill some of the gaps in air trans-
port research emanating from developing countries. These have
been lean in the pool of global air transport research; It also
highlights critical safety issues in the industry for further research
and policy action, such issues are oftenmore fundamental than that
which obtains in developed countries. A background on air trans-
port industry operations in Africa and specifically Nigeria, enables
us to situate the safety challenge issues appropriately.

2. Air transport in Africa

Sequel to two decades of slow progress, Africa’s economic
growth performance has improved substantially since the start of
the 21st century. The continent’s growth story is however punc-
tured by the fact that commensurate development in the form of
positive structural economic change, increase in number of jobs
and higher disposable income have not often been associated with
the salutary growth figures emanating from the continent. As such,
many African economies still depend heavily on commodity pro-
duction and exports with little value addition and few forward and
backward linkages to other sectors of the economy.Major deficits in
physical, institutional and policy infrastructure have further
contributed to the continent’s development challenge.

In spite of the unfriendly economic context within which Afri-
ca’s air transport businesses operate, the region has witnessed
some growth in its air transport industry. African airlines passenger
traffic grew by 12.9 percent while cargo went up by 23.8 percent in
2010 compared to the previous year. African airlines passenger load
factors also increased from 67.8 percent in 2004 to 70.2 percent in
2008. It however reduced to 69.1percent in 2010 and was lower
than the global industry average of 75.7%. In the first quarter of
2011, African airlines passenger traffic grew by 7.20% (Chingosho,
2011). As noted by Rhoades (2004), ‘traffic increases, interna-
tional funding and support of safety initiatives, airline privatization
and liberal bilateral agreements have all combined to produce
potential new growth and development in African civil aviation
which has historically lagged far behind that of the rest of the
world’.

As with other regions of the world, air transport has become
vital in Africa’s socio-economic development. It is equally critical
for international trade, tourism and for regional integration. Africa’s
aviation industry supports about 6.7 million jobs-equivalent to
$67.8b in economic activity (IATA, 2013). The industry connectivity
links the continent to global markets and opens up economic op-
portunities. However, the majority of people with transport needs
are excluded from the use of airlines and airline services because
these services prove to be expensive given prevailing low levels of
disposable income among Africans. Due to the fact that the use of
air transport is restricted to the few in the middle and high income
brackets therefore, the industry does not enjoy amarket share large
enough to foster the catalytic effects that the air transport industry
is normally capable of achieving in an economy.

Currently, about 10 percent of Africans travel by air (ADB, 2012);
but given the current rate of economic growth and an emerging
middle class, high demands for air transport are envisaged. The
African Development Bank (ADB) group forecasts for the period
2010e2015 that Africa will be the third fastest growing region in
the world in terms of international traffic with an average growth
rate of 6.1 percent compared to the global average of 5.8 percent.
The trend is expected to persist in the coming years due to perva-
sive factors of increasing urbanization and the emergence of the
middle class. Given this expectation, the need to improve safety
levels in the region has become crucial.

It should be noted that there are wide differentials among Af-
rican countries with respect to growth indices in air transport.
South Africa, Mauritius, Kenya and Ethiopia for examples have been
successful in establishing strong and viable air transport industries.
The common features in these model countries are heavy in-
vestments in air transport infrastructure and objectively imple-
mented liberalization policies including participation in alliances
and code-share agreements which increase commercial freedoms
for the nation’s main airlines. Flag carriers in these countries are
thus more competitive and also exhibit enviable safety records in
the continent.

Nigeria has however been less successful in the establishment of
a solid and vibrant air transport industry. In some airports, basic
infrastructures such as perimeter fences are lacking, and for some,
access roads are in poor conditions. There have also been recurring
issues of poor Air Traffic Control (ATC). Moreover, airlines debt
profiles are unsavory and in many cities, users decry the state of
airport facilities. While the country’s airline deregulation process
appears to have been properly implemented, the subsequent
liquidation of its national carrier was linked intricately with poli-
tics. The fall outs of this process remain largely unresolved. This
sub-optimal operating environment invariably impinges on safety
levels observed in the nation’s aviation industry.

3. Methods

3.1. Data and sources

The study made use of data on air accidents which involved
Nigeria registered aircraft during the period 1985e2010. The year



A.Y. Daramola / Journal of Air Transport Management 35 (2014) 39e50 41

Fig. 1. The HFACS framework.
Source: Wiegmann and Shappell (2003).
1985 was chosen as base year for this study because it signifies the
formalization of deregulation in Nigeria’s airline sector. Passenger
movement has soared since deregulation and safety concerns have
equally become more paramount with the entry of several private
airlines. The data, which also included figures on fatalities were
sourced from the Accidents Investigation Bureau (AIB) of the
Nigerian civil Aviation Authority (NCAA) and from the Aviation
Safety Network (ASN) database. Many of the accident data had
accident reports attached, which were used in the study. Baseline
data on passengers and volumes of aircraft movement were ob-
tained from the Federal Airports Authority of Nigeria (FAAN).

Interviews were conducted with airlines aviation industry offi-
cials (NCAA, AIB, FAAN and Nigerian AirspaceManagement Agency-
NAMA). The out from their expert assessments formed the primary
data for the study.

3.2. Trend analysis

Data on passenger movement, aircraft movement and air acci-
dent occurrences were used to calculate accidents and fatality rates.
Conventionally, the ICAO calculates passenger fatalities as a rate of
100 million passenger kilometers and accidents as a rate of 100,000
landings or 100 million km flown. For Nigeria, data on aircraft and
passenger movement are readily available, while data on aircraft
and passenger km traveled may be rather scarce; hence accidents
were calculated as a rate of 100,000 flights; while passenger fa-
talities were calculated as a rate of 100,000 passengers moved. The
global figures were recalculated using the same exposure variables
as denominators to make for meaningful comparisons.

4. Theory and calculations

The Human Factors Analysis and Classification System (HFACS)
was used as the analytical framework for the accident reports. The
HFACS appears to be very applicable in aviation accident analysis as
observed in literature (Shappell and Wiegmann, 2001, 2003, 2004;
Wiegmann and Shappell, 1997, 2001a,b,c, 2003; Li et al., 2008). The
HFACS was developed from Reason’s organizationally based model
of human error (Reason,1990,1997) and provides an organizational
framework for accident analysis. The framework is currently rele-
vant and is becoming increasingly applicable because vehicles,
equipment andmachinery used in aviation (as in other sectors), are
now technologically advanced and safety assured. Consequently,
many accidents these days are traced to the users rather than the
machines.

The HFACS addresses human errors and the factors underpin-
ning them at four different levels. The framework is described
diagrammatically in Fig. 1. Level 1 (unsafe acts of operators-active
failures) is the level at which the majority of accident in-
vestigations have been focused in the past. These are the behaviors
of the flight crew on the flight deck that contribute directly to the
accidents; usually referred to as pilot error. Failures at this level are
further classified into 2 sub-categories: errors and violations. Vio-
lations are instances of willful disregard of rules which subse-
quently result in an accident. An example is failure to perform
mandatory checks or maintenance on aircraft. Level 2 (pre-
conditions for unsafe acts-latent/active failures) addresses the
psychological pre-cursors to the active failures at level 1, such as the
substandard conditions of the operators and the operating envi-
ronment which predispose them to making an error. Level 3 (un-
safe supervision-latent failures) traces the causal chain of events
producing the unsafe acts up to the level of the front-line super-
visors. Level 4 (organizational influences-latent failures) describes
the contributions of fallible decisions in upper levels of manage-
ment that directly affect supervisory practices, as well as the
conditions and actions of front-line operators. Each higher level
affects the next downward level in the HFACS framework.

Although the HFACS was originally developed and tested within
the U.S military, the framework has reliably accommodated human
causal factors associated with civil aviation accidents as well
(Wiegmann and Shappell, 2001b). Nevertheless, there has been
some criticism of the framework. Most importantly, Beaubien and
Baker (2002) noted that it was often difficult to obtain informa-
tion about the latent conditions (unsafe supervision and organi-
zational influences for examples) from accident reports. Apart from
the absence of details on latent issues, the other limitation in the
use of the HFACS framework is the subjective classification of issues
into the various categories especially when the reports are being
assessed by a single person. The output from the interviews con-
ducted in the primary survey carried out in this study is expected to
make up for these gaps as some of the issues addressed by the
respondents helped to shed light on the contents of the accident
report and the analysis of results there from.

Another shortfall of the HFACS stems from the fact that accident
report systems are often designed and employed by engineers and
front line operators. These systems therefore prove useful for
identifying engineering and mechanical failures but may not be as
competent for revealing human errors. Identifying contributory
human errors in accident reports therefore requires great scrutiny
and is made easier when reporting is done based on a theoretical
framework of human error. When this is not entirely the case,



42 A.Y. Daramola / Journal of Air Transport Management 35 (2014) 39e50
analysts depend on indicative clauses in accident reports to identify
the presence of human errors.

Working round the shortfalls of the framework however, several
studies have uncovered statistical relationships describing the cause/
effect relationships between various components at the different
levels in the analysis and classification system, giving support to the
underpinning theory behind the framework (Li and Harris, 2006a,b;
Tvarnyas et al., 2006). For these reasons the HFACS framework is
adoptedas the analytical framework in this study. This studyhowever
makesamethodological contributionbyundertakingbothqualitative
and quantitative analysis of the aviation accident data for Nigeria,
botharedonealong the lineofHFACS. Thequalitative analysis isbased
on the expert assessments while the quantitative analysis derives
from the contents of the accident reports.

Using the narratives of the accident reports, relevant HFACS
categories are identified from the reported primary and secondary
causes. In many cases, more than one category was identified.
These were subjected to a chi-square test of association to verify
significant relationships in adjacent categories in the HFACS
framework.

5. Results and discussion

5.1. Passenger and aircraft movement trends

Passenger and aircraft movement (Fig. 2) form the basis of later
discussions on accident and passenger fatality rates. Fig. 2 shows
that air passenger volumes have been mostly on the upward trend
from about 1998 after the declining trend of the 1985e1989 period
and the oscillations of the 1990e1997 period. Declines in the 1985e
1989 period have been attributed to the initial industry adjustment
challenges that followed deregulation of domestic air services in
Nigeria in 1985; While the unstable nature of growth in passenger
movement between 1990 and 1997 were the result of political
instability which impacted all sectors of the economy negatively,
including air transport (Daramola, 2012). Volume of passenger
movement over the period ranged from 2.3 million (1998) to 10.7
million (2010).

Since airlines are primarily passenger movers, aircraft move-
ment trends have largely followed that of passenger movement.
Similar to the pattern for passenger movement, aircraft movement
during the period was lowest (86,837) in 1998 and highest
(206,509) in 2010.

5.2. Air accidents trend in Nigeria (1985e2010)

During the period of analysis, a total of 45 accidents were
recorded, 39 of which were classified ‘category 1’, and the other six
Fig. 2. Air passenger and aircraft movement trend-Nigeria.
Source: FAAN (1985e2010).
were ‘category 2’ accidents. A ‘category 1’ accident, also referred to
as ‘hull loss’, describes an accident which results in total loss of the
air plane. An accident categorized as ‘category 2’ is one which re-
sults in repairable damage to the air plane. Both categories may or
may not involve fatalities. Out of the 45 accident occurrences dur-
ing the period, 21 accidents involved fatalities; and the total
number of fatalities was 781 (Table 2).

All the aircraft involved in accidents during the period of anal-
ysis were offering domestic services. Only two of the 45 flights
were cargo flights. Both scheduled and non-scheduled passenger
flights had almost a fair share of accidents during the period of
analysis. There were nineteen accidents recorded for scheduled
passengers and eighteen accidents for non-scheduled passenger
flights, while the other flights were either military or government
owned aircraft. The non-scheduled flights include charter/execu-
tive flights, ferry and test flights, as well as flights for survey and
research. Scheduled passenger flights had 16 ‘category 1’ accidents
and three ‘category 2’ accidents, while non-scheduled passenger
flights recorded just one ‘category 2’ accident and 17 ‘category 1’
accidents. There were four military flight accidents in ‘category 1’
and one cargo flight accident also in ‘category 1’. Scheduled Pas-
senger flights had the highest number of fatalities (568) and this is
due to the fact that there are many more passengers on flights of
this nature than on any other. In all, as much as 75 per cent of the
accidents were recorded during aircraft approach and landing with
51 per cent of the accidents occurring specifically in the landing
phase of the flights while 24 per cent occurred in the approach
phase.

Fig. 3 depicts the number of accidents that occurred each year
over the period of analysis. During the 26 year period, there were
four years of reprieve in which no accidents were recorded. 1992
was the worst year in the analysis period when five accidents were
recorded. The trend in the number of accidents which occurred
during the period shows an oscillating pattern.
5.3. Aircraft types and age

Table 3 groups the aircraft in the accident list by age and by the
frequency of accidents. In all, there were 22 different aircraft types
involved in accidents during the period, but the most frequently
occurring were the BAC 1-11, and the Boeing 737e200. The mean
age for the BAC 1-11 aircraft (seven of them) on the accident list
was 25.1 years; median age was 24 years. This was higher than
what obtained for all aircraft involved in accidents during the
period of analysis which was mean age was 16.8 years, and median
age was 17 years. The BAC 1-11 was developed by the British
Aircraft Corporation (BAC) in 1960 specifically for short range
flights. It was one of the most popular British airliner designs at the
time but was widely retired in the developed economies by the
1990s. This was due to the fact that the aircraft could no longer
meet minimal noise restrictions by then (Gunn, 1999; Lewis, 2000).
Table 2

Category 1 Category 2

Non-scheduled 17 1 18 18

A.Y. Daramola / Journal of Air Transport Management 35 (2014) 39e50 43

Fig. 3. Number of aviation accidents (1985e2010).
Source: Aviation Safety Network (2010).

Fig. 4. Highest fatality counts for civil aviation accidents by aircraft age category.
Source: Based Table 3.
The BAC 1-11 was banned in Nigeria after the EAS crash of 2002
where 140 fatalities were recorded (Daily Trust, 2002). At the same
time, the government also prohibited aircraft which were above 22
years from registration date.

Age range for the B737 aircraft on the list was from 6 to 24 years
with a mean age of 13.8 years. The configuration of the B737-200
gives it a relatively short runway requirement in addition to a
greater payload; this partly explains its popularity among Nigerian
airlines at the time. Therewere 223 fatalities associatedwith the six
accidents involving B737-200 on the list. Other common aircraft
types in the list are the Boeing 727, Embraer 110, HS-125 and the
Dornier Do-228.

The average age of all active aircraft in Nigeria as contained in a
2006 inventory was 23.2 years, the oldest was 38 years. Age of
aircraft on the accident list for the period 1985e2010 showed that
aircraft age ranged from three years to 33 years. There were only
seven aircraft out of the 45 which were less than 10 years; and
these aircraft recorded zero fatalities against the accident occur-
rences encountered. Fifteen of the aircraft were in the 10e19 year
category, while most of the aircraft (17) involved in accidents
during the period were in the 20e29 year category. Fatality counts
(for civil aviation accidents) appear to have increased in range of
magnitude with increasing age of aircraft. While aircraft less than
10 years recorded zero fatalities, those ones in the 10e19 year
category had fatalities ranging from 0 to 9 for civil aviation acci-
dents. The aircraft in the 10e19 year category which had a major
fatality count of 158 was a Nigeria Air Force plane. Aircraft in the
20e29 year category had fatality counts ranging from 0 to 117.
There were two aircraft in the over 29 year category and these had
fatality counts of 108 and 143. Fig. 4 shows the highest fatality
count for each aircraft age category on the accident list.

Evidence points to the fact that average aircraft fleet age in
Nigeria is much higher than global average levels. The global fleet
according to IATA (2012) has more than tripled in the last 25 years,
but average fleet age has remained stable at about 11 years. As
noted by the United States Federal Aviation Administration (FAA),
older airplanes are prone to fatigue.

Source: Based on Table 1.
In 2010, the United States government alerted civil authorities of
structural defects suffered by aging aircraft and gavemanufacturers
and carriers in America deadlines to intensify and streamline in-
spection of metallic skeleton and skin which cause fatigue to air-
planes. According to the FAA, cracks in many aircraft in service for
two decades ormore are not reliably captured by current inspection
methods. This applies tomany of the airplanes flying in Nigeria. The
FAA noted that tiny cracks, some of them visible, often form on a
plane as it ages. Individually, the cracks are of little concern, but can
weaken an aircraft’s structure if permitted to spread and link with
other cracks. The authority thus emphasized the need to take steps
to protect thousands of jets from serious structural fatigue as they
age. Since maintenance techniques are now based on cutting edge
technology, such techniques may elude old aircraft designs and this
constitutes a serious safety breach.

5.4. Accident and passenger fatality rates

During the period of analysis, the number of aviation accidents
per 100,000 flights in Nigeria ranged from 0.48 (2010) to 3.10
(1997) for the years in which accidents occurred. Accident and fa-
tality rates in Nigeria display remarkable peaks in 1992, 1995, 1997
and in 2005, conversely, there were troughs in fatality rates in the
1986e1990 period, 1998e2001, 2003e2004 and in the 2007e2010
period as seen in Fig. 5.

Accident and passenger fatality rates follow the same trend
pattern, and as expected, peaks in passenger fatality rates have
been higher than peaks in accident rates. For the years in which
fatalities were recorded, passenger fatality rates per 100,000 pas-
sengers carried ranged from 0.03 (2008) to 4.05 (1997).

Figs. 6 and 7 attempt a comparison of global average aviation
accident and global average air passenger fatality rates1 with rates
recorded in Nigeria. It is seen that for all the years in which acci-
dents were recorded in Nigeria during the period 1985e2008, ac-
cident rates in Nigeria were higher than world average figures. The
trend in global average figures for accident rates show a consistent
decline from 0.21 in 1993 to 0.05 in 2001. There was a slight in-
crease in the figure to 0.06 in 2002, but this further declined to 0.03
in 2003.

While it is expected that variations for a single country will no
doubt be more pronounced than what is contained in the aggre-
gated global figures (as in Figs. 6 and 7), the insight here is in the
disparity in the level (higher country levels than global for much of
1 Global figures for accidents and fatalities were available up to 2008.

the years), much more than in the disparity in variation over the

safety measures by airlines and by aviation regulatory bodies.
However, there was an increase in global average figures to 0.07 in
2005 but by 2008, it had decreased to 0.05.

Passenger fatality rates have equally been higher for Nigeria
when compared with global average figures that is for the years in
which fatalities were recorded (Fig. 7). The only exception to this
was in 1985, when passenger fatality rate for Nigeria (0.12) was
lower than world average (0.13).

During the twenty-four year period (1985e2008) for which
comparisons were made, there were twelve years in which fatal-
ities were not recorded at all for Nigeria. Figures on passenger ki-
lometers performed were unavailable for Nigeria; hence it is
difficult to say whether kilometers performed by Nigerian airlines
in these years were relatively less than usual. However, aircraft
movement figures in Nigeria for the years in which no accident
occurrences were recorded were generally above the average for
the period of analysis (1985e2010). This suggests that passenger
kilometers performed may not have been less than usual levels.
Similar to the trend in aviation accident rates, Nigeria exhibits
much wider disparities in passenger fatality rates than the world
average.

 5.5. Industry experts’ assessment

Interviews were conducted with officials of domestic airlines,
officials from the Federal Airports Authority of Nigeria (FAAN), the
Nigeria Airspace Management Authority (NAMA), the Accident
Investigation Bureau (AIB) and the Nigerian Civil Aviation Authority
(NCAA). The officials spoke on the Nigerian aviation industry and
gave their assessments based on the one year period preceding the
survey. The survey was carried out in August 2009. The outputs are
presented belowand are discussed along the lines of the conceptual
framework- HFACS.

5.5.1. Domestic airlines
At the time of the survey, there were eight fully functional do-

mestic airlines operating, six of these had officials available to
respond to the interviews conducted. The airline officials identified
industry wide problems which border on their safety operations
and pose challenges for them. A highlight of the issues which also
explain the relatively high accident and fatality figures discussed in
the previous Section (5.2) are given below and elaborated upon
subsequently, they include:

 Inadequate airport facilities
 Shortage of skilled manpower
 The problem of bird strikes
 Apron congestion and,
 Poor Air Traffic Control

Some airline officials noted that the Federal Aviation Authority
of Nigeria (FAAN) is ill-equipped noting that airport facilities are
inadequate in case of emergencies. Such situations are said to
compromise the safety efforts of individual airlines. Invariably,
these situations form preconditions for the unsafe acts of operators
specifically in the areas of the physical and technological
environments.

Weather was identified as a major problem, when visibility is
low, flights are delayed and in fact, quite a number of the accidents
during the 1985e2010 period were weather related, and were due
to poor visibility. This challenge situates into the preconditions for
unsafe acts fueled by a poor technological environment. In ideal
environments, the vagaries and challenges of weather are well
taken care of in the aviation industry with appropriate technolo-
gies. The airline officials noted that proper Instrument Landing
Systems (ILS) at airports would solve the weather related problems
readily.



A.Y. Daramola / Journal of Air Transport Management 35 (2014) 39e50 45
Shortage of manpower in the aviation industry was equally
identified as a challenge which leads to high cost of operations. The
dearth of competent pilots was mentioned as an example. The few
ones available are said to demand very high prices. Many times,
airlines have to resort to using foreigners in their operations who
naturally would be paid in foreign currency. The skilled manpower
shortage translates into a safety challenge because competent
personnel are needed to effect safety operations. This depicts a
faulty organizational climate which could consequently result un-
safe acts of operators in the form of skill based errors are expected.

The problem of bird strikes was mentioned severally during the
interview sessions and this problem was linked to the ineffective-
ness of FAAN to keep the airport environment clean. As explained
by airline officials, bird strikes result from inadequate supervision
and constitute a precondition for unsafe acts in the physical envi-
ronment. The airlines officials also identified the problem of apron
congestion (physical environment) and poor air traffic control
(technological environment) as limitations to airlines’ safety oper-
ations. It was observed that aircraft parking spaces at domestic
airports are inadequate, especially at the recently completed Mur-
tala Mohammed 2 (MM2). Airline officials noted the challenge of
limited maneuvering room for aircraft at the new airport. More-
over, airline officials describe the state of Air Traffic Control as ‘poor’
noting that available radar facilities do not cover a tenth of the
Nigerian airspace. Where radar does exist, trained personnel are
few and basic equipment such as radio and navigational aids are
mostly obsolete. These inadequacies coupled with the absence of
ILS in some major airports means that pilots spend more time
trying to land which could have safety implications and also
translate to a waste of resources.

5.5.2. Federal Airports Authority of Nigeria
The Federal Airports Authority of Nigeria (FAAN) is responsible

for developing and managing airport facilities in Nigeria for safe,
secure and efficient carriage of passengers and goods. The assistant
general manager (AGM) safety observed that FAAN is not very
effective in enforcing safety standards. He noted that the larger
operating environment (referring to Nigeria) is one in which safety
is not accorded priority until an accident occurs. This disposition
naturally affects the business culture at FAAN since the organiza-
tion does not exist or function in isolation. Findings from the
interview session showed that FAAN has a safety management
systems (SMS) manual which is made available to the airlines. At
the time of field work, it was noted that the manual was yet to be
approved by the NCAA. The SMS manual has a compliance clause
for the airlines.

There were no strong sanctions in place for erring airlines at the
time as the airport certification exercise was yet to be carried out.
The FAAN official interviewed noted that without airport certifi-
cation, FAAN could not impose sanctions. Deficiencies identified in
FAAN include workplace instability (organizational climate), low
morale among staff resulting mostly from issues of overdue pro-
motions (personal readiness) and improperly articulated policies
(organizational process). These factors are said to work together to
create safety lapses in the system. In summary, FAAN operations
were described as ‘not robust enough to drive safety in the in-
dustry’. The respondent noted that when policies are not clearly
articulated, it creates loopholes for airlines to get away with
anything.

5.5.3. Nigerian Airspace Management Agency
The Nigerian Airspace Management Authority (NAMA) is the

organization charged with developing the Nigerian airspace to a
level consistent with the requirements of the ICAO standard and
operating procedures (SARPS). The agency is also to ensure
provision of air navigation services to ensure safe, efficient, expe-
ditious and economic flight operations. In compliance with its
mandate, the agency has a set vision of making the Nigeria airspace
rank among the safest airspaces in the African-Indian Ocean (AFI)
region and indeed in the world over. NAMA gets support services
from FAAN and from the Nigerian Metrological Agency (NIMET),
while NCAA performs oversight functions. The respondent from
NAMA noted that there are multiple layers of safety maintenance to
assure safety; there are equally layers of quality assurance.

Available navigational aids at airports listed include the instru-
ment landing system (ILS), the distance measuring equipment
(DME), the non-directional beacon and the very high frequency
omni-directional range (VOR). Responses from airline officials
however point to the fact that ILS at many airports are inadequate.
Communication facilities available include the very high frequency
(VHF) radioeRCAG (remote control, air to ground, ground to air and
air to air). These communication navigation surveillance and air
traffic management (CNS/ATM) are maintained by NAMA staff,
though they are not manufactured in Nigeria, NAMA has staff
trained to install, align and maintain the equipment and this, ac-
cording to the respondent is a tremendous achievement.

5.5.4. Nigerian Civil Aviation Authority (NCAA)
The NCAA was described as the watchdog in the aviation in-

dustry. The authority is fairly new, established in 1999 and
commenced operations in year 2000. The establishment of the
NCAA was in response to ICAO requirements that member states
establish an appropriate state organization charged with the
necessary powers to ensure compliance with air navigation
regulations.

The responding official at the Air Transport Regulation unit
assessed the authority as being effective in the area of safety
standards enforcement and also mentioned that full autonomy for
the authority would make it even more effective in its mandate.
Operating regulations for airlines and for other aviation agencies
are as stipulated in the ICAO Standards and Recommended Prac-
tices (SARPS). Sanctions for erring airlines include fines, suspen-
sions or outright withdrawal of operating licenses depending on
the gravity of the offence. The NCAA responding official essentially
referred other issues for the interview to the Accident Investigation
Bureau (AIB) due to the fact that the study was on accidents and
safety management systems. The AIB is a bureau under the NCAA.

5.5.5. Accident Investigation Bureau (AIB)
At the AIB, Safety standards in Nigeria’s aviation industry were

assessed as better than what obtains in the Nigerian society
generally. The AIB respondent noted that human beings play a
crucial role in the aviation industry and they have their limitations.
As such, even when automation is introduced in several aspects of
aviation, such facilities are still manned by human beings. He
identified some deficiencies in the industry as follows:

 Maintenance culture, especially inspections and checks is poor
in the aviation industry. There are daily checks, weekly, monthly
and annual checks. These omissions result from inadequate
supervision. If the checks are done as and when due, things are
likely to run well.

 Training of personnel is deficient, in aviation, training and re-
training is essential to keep abreast of existing technology
(organizational climate).

 Rest period for skilled personnel (especially pilots and engi-
neers) is insufficient as there are not enough trained hands in
these categories (Resource management). Fatigue leads to safety
compromise. Meanwhile, prevention (of an accident) is better
than cure.



46 A.Y. Daramola / Journal of Air Transport Management 35 (2014) 39e50

Fig. 8. HFACS frequency for Accident cases (%).
Source: Author’s calculation based on Aviation Safety Network (2010).
 Navigational aids and meteorological equipment could be bet-
ter. Most times, operators barely cope with the weather and
many accidents are weather related (technological
environment).

5.6. Summary of interview output

Several aviation industry challenges were identified in the or-
ganizations where interviews were conducted. Table 4 gives a
summary of these challenges and locates each in the organization
responsible for action on the issues.

5.7. Content analysis of accident reports using HFACS framework

Using the accident reports obtained from the Aviation Safety
Network (ASN) database, the presence or absence of each HFACS
category was deduced from the narrative of each accident report.
Except for three of the narratives which gave no clues as to the
conditions precipitating particular accidents, other accident reports
provided a fair idea of some relevant HFACS categories. There is
however more information on the immediate circumstances sur-
rounding the accidents, than we have for probable remote causes.
Information contained in Fig. 8 shows that skill based errors is the
most predominant of the HFACS categories and is present in 66.6
per cent of accident cases. Physical environment and inadequate
supervision are the next most common categories and are present
in 40 per cent and 35 per of accident cases respectively.

5.7.1. Most frequently occurring categories
This section elaborates on the most frequently occurring HFACS

categories derived from the analysis of the accident reports.

5.7.1.1. Skill based errors. Pilot skills are crucial and significantly
affect the outcome of events especially in takeoff and landing of
aircraft. Even with the best of supervision and organizational
checks, real time events are directly tied to the skills displayed by
pilots. The dexterity applied by pilots can affect whether or not an
accident is avoided altogether; it can also determine the magnitude
of the effect in the case of accidents which could not be avoided.
Quite a number of the accident narratives reported pilots over-
shooting the runway. There were also instances of belly landing,
gear up landing and hurried straight in approaches. Specifically,
there were as much as 75 per cent of accidents associated with
aircraft descent (51 per cent associated with landing and 24 per
cent with approach). Accident reports also show that some of the
skill based errors recorded were induced by inadequate weather
information (for example Aviation Development Corporation acci-
dent of 1997); while some were fueled by supervisory violations
(Chachangi, 1998 accident, for example).

It was noted in the course of interviews with aviation industry
personnel that training of personnel in the Nigerian aviation in-
dustry is deficient in the sense that not all personnel are exposed to
the level and frequency of training they need to attain the required
Table 4
Summary of challenges to be addressed in Nigeria’s aviation industry.

Domestic airlines FAAN

 Incompetent pilots  Poorly maintained airport environ
 Deficient training and retraining of staff making bird strikes a possible phe
 Failure to observe routine inspections and  Apron congestion

checks on aircraft  Inadequate airport facilities includ
 Inadequate rest period for staff as a result  Workplace instability and low mo

of few personnel.

Source: Field survey, 2009.
expertise required to carry out their job descriptions optimally. The
need for training and re-training was emphasized by the respon-
dent from the Accident Investigation Bureau and this can be clearly
linked with acquisition of new skills and sharpening of existing
skills.

5.7.1.2. Physical environment. Physical environment issues are
mostly hinged on weather conditions. Various combinations of
atmospheric parameters result in different weather conditions
which affect aircraft operations. Pilots require weather information
throughout the duration of the flight, but especially during the
landing and takeoff phases. This information is issued to the control
tower by the meteorological services at thirty minute intervals and
contains information on actual weather, example harmattan haze,
fog, thunderstorm or rain; runway visual range and horizontal
visibility. Other details contained in weather information include
surface wind speed and direction, clouds, atmospheric pressure
and air temperature. The industry expert assessment from AIB in-
cludes inadequate meteorological information.

When weather information is disseminated (adequate or
otherwise), it becomes the pilot’s responsibility to decide whether
the existing state of the reported parameters meets the aircraft
minima for take-off, landing or otherwise. Should the pilot make a
wrong decision based on the weather information, a decision error
occurs and this frontline error could actually lead to an accident.
Disregard for weather informationwas particularly clear in the case
of the Aviation Development Corporation accident of 2006.

5.7.1.3. Inadequate supervision. This category was present in 35 per
cent of accident reports analyzed. Many times, inadequate super-
vision is implied when the commander (crew captain) of the
aircraft gives an instruction or makes the decision himself that
leads to an accident. Examples in the list include Concorde Airlines
accident of 1990 where maintenance records in the log book were
omitted. This should have been cross checked by a supervisor. The
NAMA NCAA/AIB

ment,  Poor ATC  Lack of full autonomy for NCAA.
nomenon  Some obsolete radio

and navigational aids
ing ILS
rale of staff.



A.Y. Daramola / Journal of Air Transport Management 35 (2014) 39e50 47
Skypower Express accident of March 2000 was another instance.
Here the commander actually took over controls from the first of-
ficer before the accident occurred. Faulty decisions or instructions
on the part of supervisors may not be outright violations of rules,
but mostly are erroneous judgments.

Personnel and practices at the supervisory level are directly
linked with poor organizational processes at the highest level and
supervisory practices create the environments which predispose
frontline operators to make sound or faulty decisions at the
downward level. The errors of inadequate supervision are clearly
linked with the issue of dearth of skilled personnel and inadequate
rest periods for existing personnel mentioned in the assessments
by domestic airlines and the Accident Investigation Bureau. When
rest periods are not taken as stipulated, supervisorsmay experience
physiological and psychological stress and so scale down on stan-
dard supervisory procedures.
5.7.2. Test for significant associations between HFACS categories
The analysis of accident data using the HFACS framework shows

that only five pairs of associationwere significant between adjacent
categories. These are presented in Table 5. It should be noted
however, that data limitations would have moderated the findings
from the analysis. These limitations are with respect to the amount
of detail in the accident reports. As noted in literature, the lower
level of ‘unsafe acts’ that ultimately lead to accidents is where most
accident investigations have focused their efforts and consequently
where most causal factors are uncovered. Latent failures (at higher
levels 2, 3 and 4) unlike active failures (level 1) may lie dormant or
undetected for long periods, until they adversely affect the unsus-
pecting air crew. Consequently, they may be overlooked by in-
vestigators with even the best of intentions (Shappell and
Wiegmann, 2001).

In the same vein, aviation accidents reports for Nigeria show
that some of the HFACS categories had few instances of occur-
rences. Details on the immediate causes of accidents are more
available than we have details on remote causes. These remote
causes would often be associated with the upper levels (organiza-
tional influences) of the HFACS framework. Organizations here
would refer to airlines as well as other administrative and regula-
tory bodies in the aviation industry. Incidentally, the test for
strength of associations between adjacent categories showed sig-
nificance for some of these categories with few instances of
occurrences.

The analysis (Table 5) shows there are significant associations
between categories at HFACS level-3 ‘supervisory violations’ and
level-2 ‘personal readiness’ and ‘crew resource management
(CRM)’. Supervisory violations refer to willful disregard for existing
rules, regulations, instructions or standard operating procedures by
Table 5

derived from the analysis of 45 aviation accidents in Nigeria between 1985 and 2010.

managers during the course of their duties. These violations are
seen to affect the individuals’ readiness for optimal performance on
the job and by extension, coordination and team work issues that
impact overall performance.

‘Planned inappropriate action’ (level 3) is also significantly
associated with ‘crew resource management’ and ‘personal readi-
ness’ (level 2). Planned inappropriate actions refer to improper
management and assignment of work including aspects of risk
management, crew pairing and operational tempo. Level 2 category
‘crew resource management’ is significantly associated with level 1
‘decision errors’. Decision errors which are actions of front line
operators represent conscious, goal-intended behavior that pro-
ceeds as designed, albeit proves inadequate or inappropriate for the
situation. These errors typically manifest as poorly executed pro-
cedures, improper choices, or simply the misinterpretation and/or
misuse of relevant information. These significant associations be-
tween categories are represented as paths (signified by arrows) in
the HFACS framework (Fig. 9).

Significant associations between supervisory violations and
personal readiness as well as between CRM and decision errors are
in line with findings by Li et al. (2008) on civil aviation accidents in
China. From the analysis, Supervisory violationdCrew Resource
ManagementdDecision Error is the only significant path that runs
up to the front line operator level (level 1).

6. Study findings and previous research

Following the original application of the HFACS to military
aviation operations, Wiegmann and Shappel (2001a, b) have veri-
fied the applicability of the HFACS to civil aviation accidents.
However, there is not much study on the patterns and strengths of
relationships between the components of the framework. Notable
among the few studies in this regard are those by Li (2006), Li et al.
(2008), Wiegmann and Shappel (2001a, b).

In their research, Li et al. (2008) found statistically significant
relationships linking faulty decisions in upper management levels
to unsafe supervisory practices, thereby creating the psychological
preconditions for unsafe acts. Consequently, the performance of
front line operators (flight crew) is affected and accidents occur.
This was similar to the findings on military accidents in 2006
(Li, 2006). In the current study, accident paths were traced as far as
the supervisory level but not up to the upper management level.
The reason for this as stated earlier would be due to lack of detail in
the Nigerian reports.

In their study focused on assessing the utility of the HFACS
framework as an error analysis model, Wiegmann and Shappel
used aviation accident records maintained by the National Trans-
portation Safety Board (NTSB) for the period 1990e1996. Their
findings show that the highest percentage of all air crew related
accidents were associated with skill based errors, much in line with
the findings of the current study. The congruence in findings sug-
gests that skill in airplane operations handling and in decision
making is extremely crucial for improved safety in the aviation
industry.

In the United States Department of Defence Human Factors (HF)
Taxonomy, skill based errors, along with decision and perceptual
errors constitute ‘errors as failures’ in themselves. This category of
error differs from ‘error as the cause failures’ and from ‘error as a
process’. Error as the cause of failures has to do mostly with defi-
cient supervision, while error as a process pertains to deviations
from standard procedures. Standardized systemic procedures and
laid down sanctions will often help check deficient supervisions
and deviations from protocol, so that associated errors can be
greatly minimized. However, skill based errors and others in same
category are often linked to individualized actions and decisions

Fig. 9. Significant paths in HFACS categories based on the analysis of aviation accidents in Nigeria.
Source: Based on Table 4.
which may be based on operators’ discretion. The solution is to
provide training to improve proficiency, and expertise. However,
how much of these are imbibed depends more on the aptitude of
individuals receiving the training. This is where skill and skill based
errors become crucial in influencing safety in aviation. Flight crew
members particularly are more involved with issues of skill than
other airline staff as they make decisions and act in real time.

Theoretically, what this means is that in Reason’s organiza-
tionally based model of accident causation, (also called the Swiss
Cheese Model), the ability to block the hole at the level of unsafe
acts of operators holds a vital key to addressing human errors in
aviation accidents (see Fig. 10). Assuming that a conducive envi-
ronment is set for the occurrence of an accident at the higher levels
of the HFACS framework (levels 1e3), it still cannot occur except
there is an unsafe act by an operator. It remains to proven if in fact
an unsafe act alone would not lead to an aviation accident even
when there are no latent conditions at upper levels.

7. Summary

There were a total of 45 accidents in Nigeria during the period
1985e2010, 39 of which were grouped as ‘category 1’or ‘hull loss’.
Twenty one of the accidents involved fatalities which totaled 793.
There were five different accident occurrences in 1992 and this was
the highest number of accidents recorded in one single year. The
highest number of fatalities recorded in one single year (225) was
in 2005. Relative to world average figures, accident and passenger
fatality rates are higher in Nigeria for those years in which Nigeria
recorded accidents and passenger fatalities.

Some of the challenges to safety operations observed by airline
operators include inadequate airport facilities, absence of timely
meteorological information and inadequate navigational aids such
as Instrument Landing Systems (ILS). Other issues that came up in
the survey include the dearth of competent Nigerian pilots which
compels airlines to hire foreigners that demand higher pay. The
problems of bird strike, apron congestion and poor Air Traffic
Control (ATC) were also mentioned.
Respondents from the administrative and regulatory bodies in
the aviation industry corroborated some of the issues raised by the
airline operators. It was noted at the time of the survey that the
Federal Airports Authority of Nigeria (FAAN) was not effective in
enforcing safety standards at airports and that its operations were
mostly reactive (measures are put in place after the occurrence of
an accident) rather than preventive. The Nigeria Airspace Man-
agement Agency (NAMA) maintains that navigational aids and
communication equipment are available, but airline operators are
of the opinion that these are mostly inadequate. Some other chal-
lenges in the Nigerian aviation industry were identified at the Ac-
cident Investigation Bureau as irregular inspections and checks of
aircraft and personnel, deficient training of personnel and shortage
of skilled personnel in the airlines which leads to the few available
ones being overworked. These challenges are all observed to
compromise safety standards.

The content analysis of the accident reports for the period
(1985e2010) using the Human Factors Analysis Classification Sys-
tem (HFACS) shows that many of the accidents are associated with
skill based errors, physical environment and inadequate supervi-
sion. The findings on the preponderance of skill based errors sug-
gest that individual aptitude, proficiency and expertise of those
directly involved in flight operations does have much weight and
influence on accident and safety records in air transport operations.

The analysis of the accident reports shows there were five pairs
of significant associations between adjacent categories in the
HFACS framework. An example is that between level 3 (Supervisory
Violations) and level 2 (Personal Readiness and Crew Resource
Management). The analysis also showed a significant association
between Crew Resource Management (level 2) and Decision Errors
(level 1).

8. Conclusions and recommendations

The relatively higher levels of accidents and passenger fatality
rates in Nigeria compared with global average levels are not
desirable and needs to be checked. Findings from the survey and



A.Y. Daramola / Journal of Air Transport Management 35 (2014) 39e50 49

Fig. 10. Reason (Swiss Cheese) model of accident causation.
Source: Reason (1990).
from the analysis of the accident reports point to the need to
address personnel skill, physical environment issues (mostly
weather related) and supervisory competence. Training and re-
training of pilots and engineers must therefore be priority for
airline operators and must never be compromised. Challenges in
the physical environment category are to be handled by Federal
Airports Authority of Nigeria and the Nigerian Airspace Meteoro-
logical Agency. Adequate ILS and timely meteorological informa-
tion must be emphasized. Inadequate supervision may well be
linked to the same issues of training deficiencies. If captains on the
aircraft are not very competent, then supervision cannot be
adequate. Both the first officers as well as the supervisory officers
on the plane must be skillful in handling situations which arise in
flight. The combination of physical environment problem (which is
a precondition for unsafe acts of operators) coupledwith skill based
deficiencies creates a conducive environment for accident occur-
rences. Although statistical tests do not show a significant associ-
ation between these two categories, the fact that they form part of
the most frequently occurring categories in the Nigerian landscape
means that measures should be put in place to curb these problem
areas.

Specifically, Nigerian airlines need to check supervisory viola-
tions, crew resource management and decision errors which
appear to form the most potent path for accident occurrence as
observed from the analysis of the accident reports. This path fol-
lows significant associations between adjacent categories of the
HFACS framework and is the only that runs all the way to level 1-
where actions leading to accidents actually occur.

Administrative and regulatory bodies in Nigeria’s aviation in-
dustry (FAAN, NAMA, NCAA and AIB) must change their manage-
ment styles from reactive to pro-active and preventive for cost
effective safety administration and regulation. Safety operations
will be compromised when the financial implications of such
become so huge as to threaten airlines break even points. Avoidable
losses and expenses therefore need to be curbed. To this end, FAAN
needs to put adequate lightning facilities in place at all airports.
Sanitation of airport environs could then be carried out in the night
hours as suggested, so as to minimize creating conducive envi-
ronments for the occurrence of bird strikes which is said to cost
airlines huge amounts of money. More extensive sanitation could
actually help remove bird’s roosting sites altogether.

Airlines may need to invest in training of local personnel to
avoid the ultimate need for hiring foreign experts. Such training
could come with the clause of bonding the staff so trained for a
specified number of years. In the long run this will result in cost
saving as local staff will be paid in local currency. Should it become
clear that airlines are unable to break even after cutting out
avoidable expenses, there may be a need to consider mergers. This
will help pool resources so as to be able to bear the compulsory
costs of safety operations and still be assured of a convenient profit
margin. These recommended actions, when taken by airlines and
aviation regulatory bodies will help ensure that the International
Aviation Safety Audit (IASA) ‘Category 1’ certification status ach-
ieved in August 2010 is maintained.

The author acknowledges funding support from the Nigerian
Institute of Social and Economic Research (NISER) to carry out the
study from which this paper has been drawn.

vii 

Resumo 

 

 

 

 

Título do Projeto 

Análise do Trabalho Cognitivo dos Pilotos de Helicóptero da Aviação Offshore na 
Bacia de Campos 

Resumo 

Este trabalho tem como objetivo identificar os fatores contribuintes e os 
constrangimentos que interferem na atividade dos pilotos que voam na Bacia de 
Campos a serviço da Petrobras, e cujas conseqüências afetam o desempenho 
operacional do sistema de transporte como um todo, bem como a segurança de vôo por 
um lado, e as condições de trabalho dos pilotos, por outro. 
A pesquisa utiliza a metodologia da análise cognitiva do trabalho, de tradição européia 
(Guérin, et al) articulada às recentes abordagens de engenharia cognitiva de sistemas 
cognitivos desenvolvidas pelos professores David Woods e Erik Hollnagel (Hollnagel & 
Woods, 2005). 

Palavras-Chave 

Análise da atividade, análise cognitiva do trabalho, atividade dos pilotos de helicóptero, 
segurança do trabalho, segurança de vôo. 



viii 

Abstract 

 

 

 

 

Project Title 

Analysis of the Cognitive Work of Campos Basin Offshore Aviation Helicopter Pilots 

Abstract 

The objective of this work is to identify the contributing factors and constraints that 
interfere in the work activity of helicopter pilots flying for companies rendering 
offshore aviation services to Petrobrás in Campos Basin, and whose consequences affect 
the operational performance of the system as a whole, as well as flight safety on one 
hand, and pilot’s work conditions on the other. 
The research uses work analysis methodology of European tradition (Guérin, et al) 
coupled with recent cognitive systems engineering approaches developed by professors 
David Woods and Erik Hollnagel (Hollnagel & Woods, 2005). 

Keywords 

Activity analysis, cognitive work analysis, helicopter pilot’s activities, work safety, 
flight safety.

15 

Cap.  1 – Introdução 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Figura 1 – “Helicóptero” de Leonardo Da Vinci 
( fonte: site www.leonardo.net/flying.html ) 

A idéia de voar dominou a mente dos grandes pensadores da humanidade.  Ícaro e 

Dédalo experimentaram, na mitologia, um vôo com asas de mentira coladas com cera.  

No final do século XV o gênio Leonardo da Vinci já apresentava esboços engenhosos 

sobre máquinas de voar.  Da Vinci, no entanto, ainda não possuía um embasamento 

teórico para dar vida às suas invenções.  Tal suporte técnico surgiria mais tarde com o 

aperfeiçoamento e desenvolvimento da física e engenharia.  Foi Santos Dumont que em 

1905 realizou a primeira decolagem com uma máquina de vôo e entrou para a história 

como o pai da aviação.  Com o avanço do século XX, impulsionada em parte pela 

indústria bélica, a aviação se desenvolveria e assumiria enorme importância no 

progresso da humanidade.  Dumont morreu em desgosto ao ver sua invenção 

transformada em máquina de guerra, no entanto, o advento do transporte aéreo foi 

fundamental para encurtar as distâncias e estreitar as relações humanas. 

Nos dias atuais, o sucesso do modelo de exploração de petróleo em águas profundas 

vem gerando uma enorme demanda de funcionários que trabalham em alto-mar, 

embarcados nas muitas plataformas existentes no país.  Para suprir a necessidade de 

deslocamento dessas pessoas do continente até as plataformas, foi designada uma frota 



16 

enorme de helicópteros.  Embora esses helicópteros operem dentro de rigorosos padrões 

de segurança e manutenção aplicados à toda a aviação em geral e à aviação de 

helicópteros em particular, foi observado, nos últimos tempos, a necessidade de uma 

avaliação específica para esse tipo de vôo, denominado vôo offshore. 

Este trabalho tem como objetivo identificar os fatores contribuintes e os 

constrangimentos que interferem na atividade dos pilotos que voam helicópteros na 

Bacia de Campos a serviço da Petrobras, e cujas conseqüências afetam o desempenho 

operacional do sistema de transporte como um todo, bem como a segurança de vôo por 

um lado, e as condições de trabalho dos pilotos, por outro. 

Esta análise poderá vir a subsidiar a busca de novos procedimentos que aumentem ainda 

mais a segurança desses vôos, tais como:  desenvolver um escalonamento da demanda 

de vôos de forma a ordenar e otimizar as viagens desses helicópteros, buscar novos 

meios de adequar as necessidades dos pilotos, passageiros e empresa de petróleo à 

legislação que afeta o aeronauta, etc. 

A pesquisa utiliza a metodologia da análise cognitiva do trabalho, de tradição européia 

(Guérin, et al, 2001) articulada às recentes abordagens de engenharia cognitiva de 

sistemas cognitivos desenvolvidas pelos professores David Woods e Erik Hollnagel 

(Hollnagel & Woods, 2005).  Complementando estas abordagens , as considerações 

metodológicas discutidas em outras fontes (Woods, 2003; Hoffman, 1998; Crandall, 

2006) enriqueceram a compreensão sobre o processo e ampliaram a percepção do 

espaço metodológico. 

Neste capítulo apresentamos uma visão geral do trabalho e da organização da sua 

apresentação: 

No capítulo 2 – Motivação – justificamos a escolha do tema deste trabalho.  

Apresentamos uma macro visão do setor petrolífero, das atividades de exploração e 

produção de petróleo, e do sistema de transporte aéreo que as apóiam, incluindo uma 

visão da dinâmica do seu crescimento, usando estatísticas do setor.  Relatamos acidentes 

ocorridos e a importância de se estudar este domínio. 

No capítulo 3 – Metodologia – descrevemos a Análise Ergonômica do Trabalho com 

foco na atividade dos pilotos, método que adotamos para desenvolver este trabalho e 

que aplica a Análise Cognitiva do Trabalho de tradição européia articulada às recentes 



17 

abordagens de engenharia de sistemas cognitivos desenvolvidas pelos professores David 

Woods, Erik Hollnagel, e outros (Woods, 2003; Hollnagel & Woods, 2005; Hoffman, 

1998; Crandall, 2006). 

No capítulo 4 – Estudo de Campo: Coleta de Dados – descrevemos e quantificamos o 

trabalho que realizamos de ir a campo coletar dados sobre a atividade dos aviadores 

(pilotos e copilotos) que voam na Bacia de Campos.  Descrevemos a amostra que 

estudamos e a evolução do processo de entrevistas que usamos para eliciar 

conhecimentos tácitos sobre a atividade de pilotar helicópteros na Bacia de Campos.  

Apresentamos um resumo das observações coletadas. 

No capítulo 5 – Análise e Resultados – descrevemos os constrangimentos aos quais os 

aviadores estão submetidos, as condições de exceção com as quais têm que lidar, suas 

estratégias para viabilizar a sua atividade, e as divergências entre as atividades 

prescritas e as praticadas.  Apresentamos diagramas de atividade das atividades dos 

aviadores, organizados para evidenciar a carga sobre os pilotos e copilotos e as classes 

de atividades que os ocupam. 

No capítulo 6 – Conclusão – descrevemos o que foi possível saber sobre os 

constrangimentos que afetam a atividade de pilotar helicópteros na Bacia de Campos e 

sobre a aplicação da metodologia adotada à este tipo de problema.  Também arrolamos 

problemas em aberto e futuras linhas de pesquisa. 

Concluímos com uma Relação Bibliográfica e Apêndices. 



18 

Cap.  2 – Motivação 

A exploração e produção de petróleo offshore cria desafios logísticos de todos os tipos.  

Principal dentre estes figura o transporte de pessoal, um serviço onde, na Bacia de 

Campos, os helicópteros dominam.  O desenvolvimento acelerado dos campos de 

petróleo da Bacia de Campos, no sudeste brasileiro, aliado ao abandono do transporte de 

passageiros por catamarã, devido às pressões dos sindicatos, promoveu o crescimento 

acentuado recente no transporte aéreo de passageiros por helicóptero.  O sistema de 

transporte aéreo, composto de elementos que vão desde a infraestrutura aeroportuária e 

os serviços de controle de tráfego aéreo até as aeronaves de asa giratória e as 

companhias que as operam, e os contratos de serviço destas companhias com a 

Petrobras, assim como os procedimentos operacionais existentes, tem conseguido 

acompanhar o crescimento, quando visto sob a ótica do volume, mas temos dúvidas 

quanto à qualidade deste crescimento.  Nos parece possível que a evolução escalar 

realizada deva ser acompanhada por mudanças estruturais no sistema, para assegurar 

sua resiliência na sua atual escala operacional e além. 

2.1 – Objetivos 

Este projeto se propôs a: 

 Analisar a atividade dos pilotos e copilotos de helicópteros no transporte aéreo de 

passageiros e cargas entre o continente e as plataformas de exploração de petróleo 

da Bacia de Campos 

 Diagnosticar e detalhar constrangimentos existentes 

 Apontar soluções visando minimizar tais constrangimentos, aumentando a 

segurança, o conforto e a qualidade dos vôos 

A relevância destes objetivos pode ser compreendida entendendo o contexto da 

atividade, suas dimensões, sua situação atual, e as consequências de falhas.  O contexto 

é a atividade petrolífera, de importância estratégica para o país.  A atividade não só é de 

grande monta (o transporte de passageiros consumiu US$176 milhões em 2006, 22% 

dos custos logísticos da Petrobras na região) como é crescente, mas se desenvolve em 

condições potencialmente crescentemente precárias, como busca mostrar este estudo.  



19 

As falhas têm consequências que habitam o imaginário popular (daí o medo de voar, tão 

comum), mas não se limitam a perda de vida dos ocupantes das aeronaves: vão desde os 

prejuízos operacionais causados por incidentes menores até o potencial para danos 

catastróficos que poderiam ocorrer com a colisão de uma aeronave com uma unidade de 

produção, passando por elementos menos tangíveis mas não menos importantes, como a 

credibilidade junto aos passageiros, requisito para ser possível contratar o contingente 

de trabalhadores necessário offshore. 

2.2 – Exploração e produção de petróleo 

A demanda mundial por petróleo continua a crescer, e muitas das reservas descobertas e 

desenvolvidas recentemente estão localizadas em locais mais inóspitos ou inaccessíveis 

tais como em águas cada vez mais profundas e distantes do litoral.  Plataformas de 

petróleo são atualmente rotineiramente posicionadas em águas com profundidades de 

até 1500 metros e até a 100 milhas náuticas do litoral, são operadas por até 400 pessoas, 

e o número de plataformas está crescendo. 

Das reservas comprovadas de petróleo e gás existentes em 2002, 46% estavam em áreas 

com lâmina d’água de 400 a 1000 metros, e 30% em áreas com mais de 1000 metros de 

profundidade, ou seja, mais de 75% de todas as reservas brasileiras se encontravam em 

águas profundas e ultra-profundas.

20 

Novos campos tem sido descobertos nestas profundidades e em áreas mais profundas 

ainda.  O poço RO-21 no campo de Roncador, perfurado em 2003, está em água com 

1.886 metros de profundidade.  Estas reservas excedem às descobertas em terra e em 

águas mais rasas.  Assim, a fração das reservas brasileiras em águas profundas e 

ultra-profundas é crescente, e já excede 80% . 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Figura 3 – A evolução da tecnologia de águas profundas e das descobertas de petróleo 
( fonte: www.petrobras.com.br/pt/quem-somos/perfil/atividades/exploracao-producao-petroleo-

gas; data de consulta:16 de novembro de 2006 ) 

A produção converge para essa distribuição, e a produção brasileira em águas profundas 

e ultra-profundas aumentou sistematicamente de 1.7%, em 1987, até mais de 66%, em 

2002, fazendo da Petrobras a maior produtora em águas profundas do mundo.  Esta 

tendência deve continuar, visto que a Petrobras tem cerca de 65% da área de seus blocos 

exploratórios offshore em profundidades de água de mais de 400 m. 

O mapa abaixo mostra a localização das unidades de exploração e produção na Bacia de 

Campos em relação aos pontos de apoio no litoral, com indicação das distâncias aéreas 

do Aeroporto de Macaé e do Heliponto de São Tomé a eles, e com indicação das curvas 

batimétricas de 100, 500, 1.000, e 2.000 metros.  A progressão para as águas profundas 

se evidencia pelos campos ainda com poucas unidades de produção. 



21

2.3 – O Transporte Aéreo 

O transporte aéreo é o esteio do transporte de passageiros na Bacia de Campos, e sua 

contribuição para a logística da atividade petrolífera na região reconhecida.  Com a 

migração do transporte de passageiros dos catamarãs para os helicópteros estes 

passaram a ser essenciais. 

A evolução das atividades de exploração e produção para águas cada vez mais 

profundas tem implicações para o transporte aéreo.  Estas implicações atuam por três 

vias principais.  A demanda de vôos cresce tanto pelo número de unidades em operação, 

quanto pelo tamanho delas, enquanto a demanda por passageiro-quilômetros é função 

também do aumento da distância até as unidades.  A maior especialização dos 

trabalhadores também poderá implicar em aumento na demanda por transbordos, onde 

os passageiros se deslocam de uma plataforma para outra. 



22

O processo de aumento da demanda vem se fazendo sentir há tempo, e a Petrobras 

estabeleceu a operação de vôos partindo do Heliponto de São Tomé a partir de 1998 

como alternativa para o limite de capacidade no Aeroporto de Macaé, que é de 400.000 

passageiros por ano, para oferecer uma alternativa de pouso em caso de condições 

meteorológicas adversas para pouso em Macaé, e para mitigar os congestionamentos 

que podem ocorrer no Aeroporto de Macaé após um período de condições adversas ao 

vôo.  Para tanto ampliou as instalações e tem um projeto em curso para dotar São Tomé 

de capacidade para 600.000 passageiros / ano à partir de 2009.  A demanda de 

transporte de passageiros projetada pela Petrobras para 2010 é de 772.200 embarques e 

desembarques, dos quais 550.000, ou 70%, atendidos pelo Heliponto de São Tomé 

(Cerveira, 2006).

O serviço de transporte aéreo é contratado pelas empresas de petróleo das companhias 

operadoras de helicópteros.  As operadoras contratadas têm que cumprir as normas de 

um ambiente regulador rigoroso, assim como normas adicionais impostas pelos 

contratos de prestação de serviço firmados.  As condições meteorológicas podem ter 

grande influência em suas operações. 

2.4 – A Bacia de Campos 

A importância e a escala da produção da Bacia de Campos, de onde vem 80% do  

petróleo consumido no país, são visíveis nos níveis de atividade e de investimento na 

região.  Alguns números dão a exata noção do que é a Bacia:

2.5 – Aeroporto de Macaé 

O aeroporto de Macaé é o nono maior aeroporto no país, em termos de aeronaves em 

vôos domésticos (pousos mais decolagens), mas apenas o trigésimo segundo em número 

de passageiros.  A disparidade nos rankings de aeronaves e passageiros é devida ao 

número pequeno de passageiros por vôo (7 em média).  Entre 2003 e 2006 o aeroporto 

de Macaé galgou 4 posições no ranking de trânsito de aeronaves, de décimo terceiro 

para nono, desbancando o aeroportos da Pampulha (7 para 12), de Curitiba (9 para 10), 

de Porto Alegre (10 para 11), e Jacarepaguá (12 para 14). 

Em 2004 o pátio do aeroporto foi ampliado (veja a foto abaixo) para acomodar mais 

aeronaves,  e há companhias operadoras de táxi aéreo construindo e/ou expandindo 

hangares para acomodar o volume de serviço. 



25 

Este crescimento em tráfego e expansão de parte das instalações foi um dos elementos 

motivadores deste estudo, pois é nestas circunstâncias que o equilíbrio entre a demanda 

sobre os trabalhadores, os meios disponíveis a eles, e sua capacidade de manter o 

sistema operando dentro de padrões aceitáveis pode ficar comprometida, introduzindo 

fragilidades frequentemente despercebidas até ocorrer um acidente. 

No aeroporto de Macaé há 9 empresas operadoras de aeronaves de asa rotativa 

(helicópteros) e 2 de aeronaves de asa fixa.  As empresas que operam helicópteros são 

empresas de táxi aéreo, e são Aeróleo, Atlas, BHS - Brazilian Helicopter System, Castle 

Air, Emar, Helivia Aerotaxi, Líder, Omni, e Senior.  Já a Ocean Air e a Team, que 

operam aeronaves de asa fixa são empresas de vôos regulares regionais. 

Estima-se que haja aproximadamente 250 pilotos de helicóptero atuando a partir de 

Macaé.

2.6 – Acidentes 

Em relação a acidentes envolvendo helicópteros, os números são surpreendentemente 

pequenos.  Em território brasileiro, por exemplo, ao se tratar de aeronaves com 

matrícula brasileira, esse número é pouco maior que 12 registros de acidentes por ano 



27 

nos últimos 12 anos.  Entretanto, esses números não diminuem o cuidado e atenção 

indispensáveis para esse número se reduza ainda mais.  Esses dados são ainda mais 

animadores quando se verifica que o uso de helicópteros em território nacional é a cada 

dia mais freqüente. 

Com esse objetivo foi criado o CENIPA (Centro de Investigação e Prevenção de 

Acidentes).  O CENIPA, desde a década de 20, vem estudando as falhas e causas de 

acidentes, promovendo investigações desses incidentes aeronáuticos, buscando a 

apuração das responsabilidades, basicamente através do inquérito.  A análise técnico-

científica de um acidente aeronáutico confere valiosos ensinamentos para que os 

mesmos erros não se repitam.  Através desse aprendizado, elaboram-se recomendações 

de segurança, podendo até resultar em alterações nas regulamentações dos aeronautas.  

Dessa forma, o órgão realiza seu trabalho com extrema eficácia, diminuindo o número 

de acidentes e aperfeiçoando, de maneira geral, a segurança de vôo.  Em anexo, se 

encontra o sumário do formulário que se preenche como relatório de incidentes 

aeronáuticos.
